While not explicitly queried here—and since no formal policy guideline request was issued—the existing body of research suggests that expert consensus emphasizes cautious interpretation of isolated findings like retinal hemorrhages without confirming abuse.
Rather than advocating universal screening routines tied exclusively to symptom clusters like seizures plus retinal bleeding—because false positives risk stigmatization—current best practices recommend individualized assessments guided by comprehensive histories (family dynamics), physical exams including ophthalmoscopic review and neuro-imaging when indicated, laboratory testing for clotting issues or metabolic imbalances if suggested by pattern recognition—or when clinical suspicion arises due to red flags such as unexplained trauma or behavioral changes among vulnerable populations.
In essence: There isn't a standardized protocol mandating routine screenings for retinal hemorrhage upon reporting of seizures alone; instead professionals adopt a multifactorial model weighing epidemiological likelihood against biological plausibility—and prioritize ruling out organic illness first before engaging questions about environmental exposure or caregiving behavior.
Thus while awareness grows around warning signs (particularly among high-risk groups)—screenings remain targeted rather than population-wide.
That said… none of this contradicts the original position—that when both findings occur together they demand deeper scrutiny regardless of origin.
So ultimately: Our earlier conclusion stands firm—one must examine alternatives closely because ignoring them compromises quality-of-care outcomes globally.
But please note: No formal guidance currently recommends automated routine checks during standard visits unless prompted by actual presentation of symptoms requiring urgent attention.

Given this background context—and returning fully back to the core task—
The most consistent message emerging across journals published by AAP/CPSC/CDC/etc.: When seizures coincide with retinal hemorrhage in a child under two years old—if any red flag exists—is not merely about suspecting abuse but understanding whether another life-threatening condition might be silently progressing undetected behind closed doors.
